whatItDoes: Obfuscate strings using 8 different encoding methods simultaneously: hex encoding, binary, unicode escapes, HTML entities, URL encoding, base64, reverse string, and ROT13. Each result is displayed with a copy button for easy use.
whyItExists: Developers frequently need to encode or obfuscate strings for various purposes like escaping data for different formats, generating test data, or understanding how encoding works across different systems.
whoShouldUse: Web developers, security researchers, CTF players, and anyone who needs to convert text between different encoding formats quickly.
useCases: Encode a string to base64 for API authentication headers or data URLs
Convert text to HTML entities for safe embedding in HTML pages
Generate hex-encoded strings for low-level protocol debugging
Apply ROT13 for simple text obfuscation in puzzles or CTF challenges
URL-encode special characters for query strings or path components
Create binary representations for educational or debugging purposes
instructions: Type or paste the text you want to obfuscate into the Input Text area
All 8 obfuscation methods are computed and displayed simultaneously
Click the Copy button next to any result to copy the obfuscated text to your clipboard
The description badge next to each method shows a preview of what the output looks like
Results update in real-time as you type in the input field
bestPractices: Use Base64 for data URIs and API authentication headers
Use URL encoding when building query strings or encoding path parameters
Use HTML entities when inserting user-generated content into HTML templates
Remember that obfuscation is not encryption — it provides no real security
Test encoded output in the target environment to ensure compatibility
commonMistakes: Assuming base64 is encryption — it is encoding and can be trivially decoded
Forgetting to URL-encode special characters in query parameters
Using HTML entities for non-HTML contexts where they won't be interpreted
Mixing up encoding methods and using the wrong one for the target format
Not handling Unicode characters correctly when encoding to ASCII-based formats
faq: Is base64 the same as encryption? — No, base64 is an encoding scheme, not encryption. Anyone can decode base64-encoded text trivially.
When should I use ROT13? — ROT13 is mainly used for hiding spoilers or in CTF challenges. It provides zero real security.
Why does my URL-encoded string look different from what I expected? — URL encoding can vary based on what characters are encoded. This tool encodes all non-alphanumeric characters for safety.
Can I use HTML entities in JavaScript strings? — HTML entities are interpreted by browsers in HTML context, not in JavaScript strings. Use unicode escapes (\uXXXX) for JavaScript.
